methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $178M total raised proxy.

methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
QuantWare, a Series B quantum computing company, is evaluated against a sector universe of 11 peers: 3 public (IonQ, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum) and 8 private companies including Q-CTRL, Quantinuum, and others. The company has raised $178M to date.

Stage-progression analysis applies a 1.9x step-up multiplier typical of Series B advancement, yielding a $338M implied valuation. Peer-median benchmarking is not applicable here: while 7 same-sector peers exist, insufficient datapoints meet narrow filtration criteria (same-stage, adjacent-stage, or similar valuation tier)—the methodology requires a minimum of three comparable observations to avoid noise. A broad all-sector median would introduce unwarranted distortion given quantum computing's heterogeneous architectures and commercialization timelines.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $287M (low) to $389M (high), with a midpoint of $338M. This reflects a single-method foundation; the range incorporates reasonable variance around the stage-progression output. Key uncertainties include the absence of tight peer comparables at QuantWare's stage and the sector's nascent commercialization profile, which limits cross-validation through multiple independent methods. A fuller triangulation would require either adjacent-stage private transactions or narrower technical peer alignment.
